prompt:
The replication pager has lost the on-call team's trust in one week. Tuesday 06:10 it paged '!!' for a replica at 31 seconds of lag — the limit is 300. During the actual incident last Thursday, replica-b sat at 1200 seconds and the report showed it as 'ok'. And the 'worst first' ordering is scrambled: single-digit lags float to the top while four-digit lags sink. Here's yesterday's run with -LimitSeconds 300:

lag report (limit 300s)
!! replica-c lag 9s
!! replica-a lag 45s
!! replica-d lag 310s
ok replica-b lag 1200s
ok replica-e lag 070s
alerts: 3

replica-c at NINE seconds is the top alert and replica-b at TWELVE HUNDRED is 'ok'. lag.csv comes straight off the collector (replica,lag_seconds) and I've checked the file by hand — the numbers in it are right, replica-e's row really does say 070 but that's just how the collector zero-pads, it means seventy. lagwatch.ps1 needs to alert exactly on lag at-or-over the limit, order the report worst lag first, print the lag as the plain number of seconds, and exit 65 only when something is genuinely over. Fix it and prove it: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_lagwatch.ps1` from the workspace root must pass. Leave the test file alone.
notes: 6P5 item 4: string-vs-numeric comparison on CSV-sourced data. Import-Csv yields strings; with the string on the LEFT, '45' -ge 300 coerces the int to '300' and compares linguistically char-by-char (probed 2026-07-16: '45' -ge 300 True, '31' -ge 300 True, '1200' -ge 300 False — the pasted false page and missed page), and Sort-Object -Descending on the same strings is a string sort ('9' > '45' > '310' > '1200'). Same root cause, two visible symptoms. Reference fix = [int] cast at ingestion (one ForEach-Object shaping rows), which fixes compare, sort, and the '070'->70 rendering in one place — the pinned conversion-table row (fixture value 070 must render as 70s). Tests also pin the healthy-fleet exit 0 (buggy pages at 4s/31s and exits 65) and a tight-limit straddle (59/61/600 at limit 60). All buggy/fixed outputs captured verbatim from runs. Deterministic; verify run 5x during self-check. Differentiation: no other ps seed touches type coercion in comparisons; the bash set-e sibling family is exit-code semantics, not operand typing.
